stem: A 7-year-old boy presents to your GP clinic with a 1-week history of a palpable purpuric rash on his buttocks and lower legs, abdominal pain, and 24 hours of left knee swelling. He had a sore throat 2 weeks ago. He is otherwise well, has a good appetite, and no haematuria reported by parents. Examination shows palpable non-blanching purpura on extensor surfaces of legs, mild ankle and knee swelling without joint effusion, and a soft non-tender abdomen.

data_table.Examination: Palpable non-blanching purpura extensor lower limbs and buttocks; bilateral mild ankle swelling, painful left knee; soft non-tender abdomen with normal bowel sounds; no testicular involvement
data_table.Investigations: Urine dipstick clear (no blood, no protein); FBC normal platelets 380; coagulation normal; UEC normal; ASOT positive 380 U; throat swab pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate monitoring plan?

options.text: Single review in 4 weeks with FBC and creatinine to confirm resolution of symptoms before discharge
options.rationale: Insufficient surveillance; nephritis can develop weeks to months after rash and is the major long-term morbidity. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schonlein Purpura)

options.text: Weekly urinalysis and blood pressure for 4 weeks, then fortnightly for 8 weeks, then 3-monthly to 12 months
options.rationale: AU paediatric guidelines (RCH) recommend urinalysis and BP weekly for first 4 weeks (when nephritis is most likely to manifest), fortnightly to 12 weeks, then 3-monthly to 12 months. Refer to paediatric nephrology if persistent proteinuria, haematuria with red cell casts, hypertension, or rising creatinine. (Source: RCH CPG: HSP; KHA-CARI Paediatric)

options.text: Daily urinalysis at home with creatinine and proteinuria measurement performed monthly for 1 year
options.rationale: Daily home urinalysis is impractical and not evidence-based; structured schedule per guideline is preferred. (Source: RCH CPG)

options.text: Renal biopsy at 6 weeks regardless of urinalysis findings as routine screening in this presentation
options.rationale: Renal biopsy is reserved for persistent significant proteinuria, declining renal function, or atypical features; not routine. (Source: KHA-CARI)

options.text: Single urinalysis and blood pressure check at 6 months in line with general practice protocols
options.rationale: Single late check misses early nephritis when intervention may matter; insufficient. (Source: RCH CPG)

explanation.summary: Henoch-Schonlein purpura (IgA vasculitis) is the most common childhood vasculitis (peak 4-7 years), classically following upper respiratory infection. Tetrad: palpable purpura on dependent areas (universal), abdominal pain (60-80 percent), arthralgia or arthritis (60-80 percent), and renal involvement (20-50 percent). Renal involvement (nephritis with haematuria, proteinuria, hypertension) is the major long-term concern, occurring weeks to months after rash onset, and is the determinant of long-term outcome. Standard monitoring: urinalysis and BP weekly for 4 weeks, fortnightly to 12 weeks, 3-monthly to 12 months. Refer to nephrology if persistent proteinuria, raised BP, or rising creatinine.
key_points: Palpable purpura on dependent areas, post-infectious context
Renal involvement may develop weeks to months later - structured monitoring
Schedule: weekly 4w, fortnightly 12w, 3-monthly 12m
Steroids reduce abdominal pain duration but not nephritis incidence
explanation.pearls: Persistent proteinuria beyond 6 months from HSP onset, even at low levels, is the strongest predictor of long-term chronic kidney disease in adulthood and warrants paediatric nephrology referral for consideration of renal biopsy and immunosuppressive therapy.

subtopic_detail: Bilious vomiting in shocked neonate - malrotation with midgut volvulus
stem: A 5-day-old term infant born at 39 weeks by uncomplicated vaginal birth (birth weight 3300 g) is brought to your tertiary neonatal ED with 6 hours of bilious vomiting, abdominal distension, and decreased feeding. The baby was breastfeeding well at home. He was discharged on day 1 of life. On arrival he is pale, peripherally cool, with capillary refill 4 seconds, and a distended tender abdomen.

data_table.Vital signs: T 35.8, BP 56/35, HR 175, RR 65, SpO2 96 percent on room air, capillary refill 4 seconds
data_table.Examination: Pale, peripherally shut down, tense distended abdomen with reduced bowel sounds; no obvious external mass; testes descended; no perianal abnormality
data_table.Investigations: BSL 2.4 mmol/L; ABG pH 7.20, bicarbonate 12, lactate 5.8; Hb 110, WCC 8.2, platelets 90; UEC: Na 132, K 5.8, urea 8.2, creatinine 56; CRP 18; clotted blood for cross-match sent; abdominal radiograph shows non-specific gas pattern with no obvious pneumatosis or free air; nasogastric aspirate is bile-stained

lead_in: What is the single most important immediate management priority?

options.text: Commence empiric intravenous gentamicin, ampicillin and metronidazole and observe the abdomen at frequent intervals while monitoring vital signs
options.rationale: Frequent observation in a shocked neonate with bilious vomiting is unsafe; volvulus may be present and demands immediate exploration. (Source: RCH CPG: Bilious Vomiting in Neonate)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Bilious Vomiting in the Neonate

options.text: Commence broad-spectrum antibiotics and arrange contrast upper gastrointestinal series within 2 hours to confirm diagnosis
options.rationale: Upper GI contrast study delays definitive surgical management in a shocked infant; bilious vomiting in neonate is malrotation with midgut volvulus until proven otherwise and warrants exploration; imaging is reserved for stable infants. (Source: RCH CPG)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Bilious Vomiting in the Neonate

options.text: Insert nasogastric tube, commence intravenous fluids and broad-spectrum antibiotics, and admit for clinical observation overnight
options.rationale: Conservative management with observation in a shocked infant with bilious vomiting risks bowel necrosis from volvulus; surgical consultation is the immediate priority. (Source: RCH CPG)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Bilious Vomiting in the Neonate

options.text: Administer 20 mL/kg fluid bolus, commence broad-spectrum antibiotics, and arrange CT abdomen with contrast to clarify the cause
options.rationale: CT abdomen exposes the neonate to significant radiation and delays surgery; not first-line investigation in suspected midgut volvulus in a shocked infant. (Source: RCH CPG)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Bilious Vomiting in the Neonate

options.text: Resuscitate with fluid and blood products, give broad-spectrum antibiotics, and arrange immediate paediatric surgical consultation for laparotomy
options.rationale: Bilious vomiting in any neonate is malrotation with midgut volvulus until proven otherwise. In a shocked infant, imaging delays surgery; the standard of care is resuscitation, broad-spectrum antibiotics, immediate paediatric surgical consult and operative exploration without waiting for upper GI contrast. Delayed exploration risks total midgut infarction within 4-6 hours. (Source: RCH CPG; APSA; ANZNN)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Bilious Vomiting in the Neonate

explanation.summary: Bilious vomiting in any neonate is a paediatric surgical emergency that must be assumed to be malrotation with midgut volvulus until proven otherwise. Volvulus produces midgut ischaemia from torsion around the superior mesenteric artery axis; total midgut infarction can occur within 4-6 hours. Stable infants undergo upper GI contrast study (definitive: abnormally positioned duodenojejunal flexure right of midline, corkscrew duodenum). Shocked or rapidly deteriorating infants go directly to laparotomy. Necrotising enterocolitis (typical in preterm, days 2-3 of life, pneumatosis on AXR) is a differential but the diagnostic approach in a shocked term neonate with bilious vomiting prioritises surgical exploration over imaging.
key_points: Bilious vomiting in neonate = volvulus until proven otherwise
Shocked infant = immediate laparotomy, no imaging delay
Stable infant = urgent upper GI contrast series
Window to prevent total midgut infarction: 4-6 hours
explanation.pearls: The Ladd procedure for malrotation involves derotating the volvulus anticlockwise, dividing Ladd's bands, broadening the mesentery, returning the small bowel to the right and colon to the left of the abdomen, and performing prophylactic appendicectomy because the appendix is left in the left upper quadrant.

sources.label: RCH CPG: Bilious Vomiting in the Neonate

subtopic_detail: NCSP intermediate-risk pathway with HPV other oncogenic and LSIL
stem: A 32-year-old G2P2 woman presents to your GP clinic for routine cervical screening test. She has had two prior negative CSTs (5 years and 10 years ago), normal Pap smears before that, no abnormal bleeding, regular menses, and is in a monogamous relationship with her husband. She has no past history of CIN or sexually transmitted infections. She is current with vaccinations including HPV.

data_table.Examination: Cervical examination normal at speculum, smooth, no friability
data_table.Investigations: Cervical screening result: HPV-positive (non-16/18 oncogenic types - 'other oncogenic HPV'); reflex liquid-based cytology: low-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion (LSIL); no high-grade cellular changes

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Routine recall for repeat cervical screening test in 5 years given negative prior screening pattern
options.rationale: Routine 5-year recall is for HPV-negative results; HPV-positive non-16/18 with LSIL needs intermediate-risk follow-up. (Source: NCSP National Cervical Screening Program)
source_refs: NCSP National Cervical Screening Program Guidelines

options.text: Repeat cervical screening test in 6 months as accelerated surveillance without colposcopy referral now
options.rationale: 6 months is too early for HPV clearance assessment; NCSP specifies 12-month repeat for intermediate-risk results. (Source: NCSP)
source_refs: NCSP National Cervical Screening Program Guidelines

options.text: Repeat cervical screening test in 12 months to assess for human papillomavirus persistence or progression
options.rationale: NCSP intermediate-risk pathway: HPV positive (non-16/18) with reflex cytology showing pLSIL or LSIL is intermediate risk; repeat CST in 12 months to assess HPV persistence. If still HPV-positive at 12 months, refer to colposcopy. (Source: NCSP Guidelines)
source_refs: NCSP National Cervical Screening Program Guidelines

options.text: Refer for immediate colposcopy for cervical biopsy and assessment without further interval surveillance
options.rationale: Colposcopy is reserved for higher-risk results: HPV 16/18 positive, persistent HPV at repeat 12-month, or high-grade cytology (HSIL). (Source: NCSP)
source_refs: NCSP National Cervical Screening Program Guidelines

options.text: Refer for LLETZ procedure as definitive cervical treatment without preceding colposcopic assessment now
options.rationale: LLETZ is treatment for confirmed CIN2/3 lesions after colposcopy; not appropriate at LSIL stage. (Source: NCSP)
source_refs: NCSP National Cervical Screening Program Guidelines

explanation.summary: Australia's renewed National Cervical Screening Program uses primary HPV testing 5-yearly from age 25-74. Result categories: HPV negative (5-year recall), HPV 16/18 positive (always colposcopy regardless of cytology), HPV other oncogenic types positive with reflex cytology (low-grade = 12-month repeat; high-grade = colposcopy). Persistence of HPV other oncogenic at 12-month repeat warrants colposcopy. HPV vaccination does not change screening recommendations. Self-collected vaginal samples are now accepted nationally with equivalent sensitivity to clinician-collected.
key_points: HPV positive non-16/18 plus LSIL = 12-month repeat
HPV 16/18 positive at any time = direct colposcopy
HSIL on cytology = direct colposcopy
Self-collection now accepted nationally
explanation.pearls: Self-collected HPV vaginal swabs are now offered to all eligible women in Australia regardless of risk factors as of 2022, with equivalent sensitivity for high-grade lesions; this has increased screening participation in under-screened populations including those with prior sexual assault history.

sources.label: NCSP National Cervical Screening Program Guidelines

subtopic_detail: ADIPS 75g OGTT diagnostic thresholds and pathway
stem: A 32-year-old G2P1 woman at 26 weeks gestation undergoes her routine 75g oral glucose tolerance test in your antenatal clinic. She has BMI 27 at booking, previous baby weighed 4250 g at 39 weeks with no documented gestational diabetes diagnosis at that pregnancy. There is no family history of type 2 diabetes. She is asymptomatic.

data_table.Examination: Fundal height 26 cm; foetal heart present, normal CTG; weight gain 9 kg this pregnancy
data_table.Investigations: 75g OGTT - fasting plasma glucose 5.2 mmol/L; 1-hour 9.8 mmol/L; 2-hour 8.7 mmol/L; HbA1c at booking 5.4 percent; urinalysis no glucosuria, no proteinuria; routine bloods unremarkable

lead_in: What is the appropriate interpretation and next step?

options.text: Normal OGTT results - reassure the patient and continue routine antenatal care without specific intervention
options.rationale: Results meet GDM diagnostic criteria; cannot be reassured as normal. (Source: ADIPS; RANZCOG)

options.text: Diagnose impaired fasting glucose and recommend dietary advice and exercise alone without further monitoring
options.rationale: GDM is diagnosed, not just IFG; requires structured management with self-monitoring. (Source: ADIPS)

options.text: Diagnose pre-existing type 2 diabetes - immediate metformin and endocrinology referral with twice-weekly bloods
options.rationale: Pre-existing T2DM would show HbA1c at or above 6.5 percent and fasting at or above 7 mmol/L; this pattern is gestational diabetes only. (Source: ADIPS)

options.text: Diagnose gestational diabetes, refer to diabetes-in-pregnancy clinic and commence dietary review with self-monitoring
options.rationale: ADIPS 2014 diagnostic criteria: fasting at or above 5.1, 1-hour at or above 10.0, 2-hour at or above 8.5 - meeting any one criterion at 75g OGTT diagnoses GDM. Here fasting 5.2 and 2-hour 8.7 meet criteria. Management: refer to diabetes-in-pregnancy MDT, dietary review, blood glucose self-monitoring 4x daily. (Source: ADIPS; RANZCOG)

options.text: Diagnose hyperglycaemia and recommend repeat OGTT at 32 weeks gestation without lifestyle intervention
options.rationale: Repeat without intervention misses diagnosis; treatment commenced now reduces macrosomia, shoulder dystocia, neonatal hypoglycaemia. (Source: ADIPS)

explanation.summary: Gestational diabetes mellitus is screened in Australian women at 24-28 weeks (earlier if high-risk: prior GDM, BMI 30 or higher, family history, ethnicity, PCOS, prior macrosomia). ADIPS criteria for 75g OGTT: fasting at or above 5.1 mmol/L, 1-hour at or above 10.0 mmol/L, 2-hour at or above 8.5 mmol/L; any single value meets diagnosis. Management: structured diet, exercise, blood glucose self-monitoring 4-7 times daily. Insulin or metformin if targets not met (fasting below 5.0, 2-hour below 6.7). Implications: macrosomia, shoulder dystocia, neonatal hypoglycaemia, increased lifetime maternal T2DM risk (around 50 percent within 10 years). Postpartum OGTT at 6-12 weeks to confirm resolution.
key_points: ADIPS criteria: fasting 5.1, 1-hour 10.0, 2-hour 8.5 (any one = GDM)
MDT pregnancy diabetes clinic referral
Self-monitoring 4x daily target fasting below 5.0, 2-hour below 6.7
Postpartum OGTT at 6-12 weeks (lifetime T2DM risk 50 percent at 10 years)
explanation.pearls: A prior GDM diagnosis carries around a 50 percent risk of developing type 2 diabetes within 10 years, so all women post-GDM should have postpartum 75g OGTT at 6-12 weeks and then 1-2 yearly HbA1c or FBG screening for life.

subtopic_detail: Severe HG admission with Wernicke prevention
stem: A 28-year-old G1P0 woman at 9 weeks gestation presents to your ED with 5 days of unrelenting vomiting (10-15 times daily), 5 kg weight loss from booking weight 8 weeks ago, inability to keep down fluids, and feeling weak. She has tried oral pyridoxine and ondansetron wafers at home without benefit. She has no abdominal pain, no fever, and her pregnancy was confirmed by transvaginal ultrasound at 7 weeks showing intrauterine viable pregnancy.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.6, BP 92/56, HR 118, postural drop 25 mmHg, weight loss 5 kg
data_table.Examination: Dry mucous membranes, tachycardic, no abdominal tenderness, no organomegaly
data_table.Investigations: Urinalysis 3+ ketones; Na 128, K 3.1, urea 8.4, creatinine 92; bicarbonate 18; TSH 0.2 (mildly suppressed), free T4 22 (mildly raised); LFTs ALT 78 ALP 110; FBC normal; pelvic ultrasound viable IUP single fetus

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Admit, commence intravenous saline at 4 mL/kg/hour with oral pyridoxine and metoclopramide and discharge once tolerating sips of fluid
options.rationale: Oral antiemetics are ineffective in severe HG; saline alone without K replacement risks worsening hypokalaemia. (Source: RANZCOG; AMH)

options.text: Admit, give intravenous Hartmann's with potassium replacement, intravenous thiamine 100 mg, and intravenous ondansetron 8 mg three times daily
options.rationale: Severe hyperemesis gravidarum with weight loss 5 percent or more of pre-pregnancy weight, ketonuria, dehydration and electrolyte disturbance requires admission. Management: IV Hartmann's with K replacement, IV thiamine 100 mg before any glucose, IV ondansetron 8 mg 8-hourly (Pregnancy Category B1). (Source: RANZCOG; AMH; KEMH)

options.text: Outpatient management with subcutaneous ondansetron home injection device and weekly urea, electrolytes and creatinine blood tests
options.rationale: Subcutaneous home device is not standard AU first-line; admission is warranted for severe HG. (Source: RANZCOG)

options.text: Refer urgently to endocrinology for the thyroid abnormalities before initiating any antiemetic or fluid management decisions now
options.rationale: Gestational transient thyrotoxicosis is HCG-mediated, transient, and requires no treatment unless persistent past 20 weeks with symptoms. (Source: AMH; ESA)

options.text: Commence oral metformin and reassure that hyperemesis gravidarum typically resolves by 12 weeks gestation without admission
options.rationale: Metformin has no role in HG management; ondansetron and supportive care are evidence-based. (Source: AMH)

explanation.summary: Hyperemesis gravidarum is severe pregnancy-associated nausea and vomiting causing weight loss of 5 percent or more, dehydration, ketonuria, and electrolyte disturbance, affecting 0.3-1 percent of pregnancies. PUQE score 13 or more is severe. Risk factors: previous HG, family history, multiple pregnancy, molar pregnancy. Admit if dehydrated, ketotic, electrolyte disturbance, or failure of oral antiemetics. Management: IV Hartmann's with K replacement, IV thiamine 100 mg BEFORE glucose (Wernicke prevention), antiemetics (pyridoxine, metoclopramide, prochlorperazine, ondansetron - all Category B or B1), DVT prophylaxis, psychological support. Transient hyperthyroidism due to HCG cross-reactivity resolves without treatment.
key_points: Admission criteria: dehydration, ketosis, 5 percent or more weight loss, electrolyte derangement
IV thiamine 100 mg BEFORE glucose (Wernicke prevention)
IV Hartmann's plus K replacement
Multiple antiemetic ladder: pyridoxine, metoclopramide, prochlorperazine, ondansetron
explanation.pearls: Wernicke's encephalopathy from thiamine deficiency in hyperemesis gravidarum can be precipitated by IV glucose infusion before thiamine replacement, leading to permanent Korsakoff syndrome in around 80 percent of survivors - always give IV thiamine first.

stem: A 32-year-old G3P2 woman delivers a healthy 4150 g male infant by uncomplicated vaginal birth with active third-stage management. Twenty minutes after delivery you are called to a brisk vaginal bleed estimated at 1200 mL over 20 minutes. The uterus is poorly contracted and boggy. The placenta was delivered intact and complete. There is no obvious tear. She has chronic hypertension on labetalol and her booking BP was 158/95.

data_table.Examination: Boggy fundus above umbilicus, atonic on bimanual compression; no trauma identified at speculum exam; bladder empty after catheter
data_table.Investigations: Estimated blood loss 1200 mL and continuing; Hb on booking 110; clotting screen sent; cross-match in progress
data_table.Medications: Labetalol; recent received oxytocin 10 IU IM as part of active third stage

lead_in: After bimanual uterine compression and IV access, what is the most appropriate next pharmacological step?

options.text: Administer ergometrine 250 micrograms intramuscularly as the first-line second uterotonic in primary PPH
options.rationale: Ergometrine is contraindicated in pre-existing hypertension; risk of severe hypertension and stroke. (Source: AMH; RANZCOG PPH)

options.text: Administer misoprostol 800 micrograms per rectum as the first-line additional uterotonic in primary PPH
options.rationale: Misoprostol PR is an adjunct, second or third-line; first-line additional is IV oxytocin escalation. (Source: RANZCOG)

options.text: Administer intravenous tranexamic acid 1 g as the immediate first-line agent before any further uterotonic
options.rationale: Tranexamic acid 1 g IV is given alongside (NOT instead of) uterotonics within 3 hours of PPH onset; not the immediate sole next step. (Source: WOMAN trial; RANZCOG)

options.text: Administer intravenous carboprost 250 micrograms in haemodynamically unstable PPH as the first-line agent
options.rationale: Carboprost 250 micrograms is IM (not IV) and contraindicated in asthma; not the first-line additional uterotonic. (Source: AMH; RANZCOG)

options.text: Administer intravenous oxytocin 5 IU bolus then 40 IU in 500 mL Hartmann's infusion as the immediate next uterotonic
options.rationale: Australian PPH algorithm (RANZCOG): in primary PPH from uterine atony, additional oxytocin IV (5 IU bolus then 40 IU/500 mL infusion at 125-250 mL/h) is first-line escalation. Ergometrine contraindicated by hypertension. Carboprost IM is second-line. Misoprostol PR is third-line. TXA 1 g IV given concurrently within 3 hours. (Source: RANZCOG; AMH)

explanation.summary: Primary PPH (blood loss over 500 mL after vaginal birth or over 1000 mL after caesarean within 24 hours of birth) is most commonly due to uterine atony (4Ts: Tone, Trauma, Tissue, Thrombin). Stepwise management: bimanual compression, IV access x2, cross-match, blood products if needed. Pharmacological ladder: oxytocin (IV 5 IU plus 40 IU infusion), ergometrine 250 mcg IM (avoid in hypertension or eclampsia), carboprost 250 mcg IM (avoid in asthma), misoprostol PR 800 mcg. TXA 1 g IV within 3 hours reduces mortality. Surgical escalation: balloon tamponade, B-Lynch suture, uterine artery ligation, hysterectomy as last resort.
key_points: PPH = over 500 mL vaginal or over 1000 mL caesarean
Oxytocin first-line; ergometrine if no HTN; carboprost if no asthma
TXA 1 g IV within 3 hours reduces mortality
Surgical escalation: balloon, B-Lynch, ligation, hysterectomy
explanation.pearls: The WOMAN trial showed tranexamic acid 1 g IV within 3 hours of PPH onset reduces death due to bleeding by approximately 30 percent, with no increase in adverse events; the benefit is lost if administered beyond 3 hours, so prioritise early TXA in any active PPH.

subtopic_detail: Rotterdam criteria diagnosis and initial management
stem: A 23-year-old woman presents to your GP clinic with 18 months of irregular cycles (every 35-65 days), acne, and increasing facial hair. She has gained 8 kg in 12 months and her BMI is 29. She is sexually active using condoms. Periods are heavy when they occur. She is not seeking conception now but wants to know her options for the future.

data_table.Examination: Hirsutism Ferriman-Gallwey score 12; moderate acne; acanthosis nigricans on neck and axillae; no thyromegaly; no virilisation
data_table.Investigations: LH 12 IU/L, FSH 6 IU/L, total testosterone 2.4 nmol/L (high-normal), SHBG 28 nmol/L (low), prolactin normal, TSH normal, free androgen index elevated, DHEAS normal, 17-OH progesterone normal; fasting glucose 5.8 mmol/L, HbA1c 5.6 percent, lipids normal; pelvic ultrasound: ovarian volume right 12 mL, left 13 mL, multiple peripheral follicles bilaterally

lead_in: Based on the Rotterdam criteria, what is the most appropriate diagnosis and initial management?

options.text: Diagnose secondary hypogonadism and refer to endocrinology for specialist input before any pharmacological intervention
options.rationale: Secondary hypogonadism would have low LH and FSH; here LH:FSH ratio is raised, typical of PCOS. (Source: ESA; AMH)

options.text: Diagnose congenital adrenal hyperplasia and refer for an ACTH stimulation test before initiating any pharmacological treatment
options.rationale: 17-OH progesterone is normal, ruling out classical CAH; LOCAH typically has higher levels. (Source: ESA)

options.text: Diagnose insulin resistance as a standalone diagnosis and commence empirical metformin 500 mg twice daily without further workup
options.rationale: Insulin resistance is a feature of PCOS but is not a standalone diagnosis; commencing metformin alone misses other diagnostic and management aspects. (Source: ESA)

options.text: Diagnose hypothalamic anovulation and recommend weight gain to restore the hypothalamic axis function
options.rationale: Hypothalamic causes typically have low gonadotrophins and low oestrogen; this presentation has hyperandrogenism and PCO morphology. (Source: ESA)

options.text: Diagnose PCOS by Rotterdam criteria, address lifestyle, and consider the combined oral contraceptive pill for cycle control
options.rationale: Rotterdam criteria (2003) require 2 of 3: oligo/anovulation, clinical or biochemical hyperandrogenism, polycystic ovary morphology on USS (20 or more follicles in either ovary or volume 10 mL or more). This patient meets all 3. Management: lifestyle (5-10 percent weight loss restores ovulation in around 50 percent), COCP for cycle and hirsutism, metformin for IR. (Source: International PCOS Network; ESA)

explanation.summary: PCOS is the most common endocrine disorder in reproductive-age women (10-15 percent prevalence). Rotterdam diagnosis: 2 of 3 - oligo/anovulation, hyperandrogenism (clinical or biochemical), polycystic ovary morphology on USS (20 or more follicles or volume 10 mL or more). Exclude mimics: CAH (17-OHP), prolactinoma, thyroid, Cushing, virilising tumour. Comorbidities: T2DM (3x risk), endometrial hyperplasia or cancer, dyslipidaemia, OSA, NAFLD, mental health. Management: lifestyle (cornerstone), COCP for cycle and androgens, metformin for IR, spironolactone for hirsutism after 6 months COCP. Letrozole first-line for ovulation induction.
key_points: Rotterdam: 2 of 3 criteria plus exclusion of mimics
USS: 20 or more follicles per ovary OR volume 10 mL or more
Annual T2DM screening (OGTT), 4-yearly endometrial USS
Letrozole first-line for ovulation induction
explanation.pearls: Endometrial protection in PCOS-related anovulation is critical: prolonged unopposed oestrogen exposure raises endometrial cancer risk around 3-fold; women with cycles over 90 days should receive cyclical progestogen (medroxyprogesterone 10 mg for 10 days every 1-3 months) at minimum even if not using contraception.

subtopic_detail: Intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis for GBS-positive woman
stem: A 28-year-old G2P1 woman at 38 weeks gestation presents to the labour ward in established labour with regular painful contractions every 3 minutes and ruptured membranes 4 hours ago at home. Her previous pregnancy was uneventful. She had GBS screening at 36+0 weeks and the result is positive (vaginal-rectal swab). She has no allergies. She wants water immersion during early labour.

data_table.Examination: Cervix 5 cm dilated, fully effaced, plus 1 station; foetal heart 140 bpm reassuring CTG; clear liquor; no maternal fever
data_table.Investigations: GBS swab at 36+0 weeks positive; FBC normal; ABO group O Rh positive

lead_in: What is the most appropriate intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis?

options.text: Intravenous gentamicin 5 mg/kg as a single dose at the time of cervix reaching 4 cm dilatation
options.rationale: Gentamicin alone does not cover Group B Streptococcus and is not the recommended agent. (Source: RANZCOG GBS Statement; eTG)

options.text: Oral cefuroxime 500 mg every 12 hours throughout labour with no IV antibiotic needed at all
options.rationale: Oral antibiotics are not used for intrapartum GBS prophylaxis; IV is needed for placental transfer to neonate. (Source: RANZCOG)

options.text: Intravenous benzylpenicillin 3 g loading dose then 1.8 g every 4 hours until delivery
options.rationale: RANZCOG and Australian Government Department of Health: IV benzylpenicillin 3 g loading dose then 1.8 g every 4 hours during labour is first-line for GBS-positive women without penicillin allergy. Cefazolin if non-severe penicillin allergy, vancomycin if severe penicillin allergy. (Source: RANZCOG; eTG)

options.text: Intravenous vancomycin 1 g every 12 hours throughout labour regardless of penicillin allergy status
options.rationale: Vancomycin is reserved for severe (immediate hypersensitivity) penicillin allergy; first-line is penicillin. (Source: RANZCOG)

options.text: Intravenous metronidazole 500 mg every 8 hours throughout labour as the first-line agent
options.rationale: Metronidazole is for anaerobic coverage, not GBS. (Source: AMH)

explanation.summary: Group B Streptococcus is the leading cause of early-onset neonatal sepsis in Australia. Universal antenatal screening at 35-37 weeks via vaginal-rectal swab. Indications for intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis: GBS-positive at this pregnancy, GBS bacteriuria this pregnancy, previous baby with invasive GBS disease, unknown GBS at term with intrapartum risk factors (under 37 weeks gestation, ROM over 18 hours, maternal fever 38 or higher). First-line: IV benzylpenicillin 3 g loading then 1.8 g 4-hourly. Cefazolin for non-severe penicillin allergy, vancomycin for severe. Adequate IAP defined as first dose 4 or more hours before delivery.
key_points: Universal antenatal GBS screening at 35-37 weeks (vaginal-rectal swab)
IAP indication: GBS positive this pregnancy or risk factors with unknown status
IV benzylpenicillin 3 g loading plus 1.8 g 4-hourly first-line
Adequate prophylaxis = first dose 4 or more hours before delivery
explanation.pearls: Cefazolin is the preferred IAP for non-severe penicillin allergy; resistance of GBS to clindamycin and erythromycin is 30-40 percent in Australia, so vancomycin is required for severe penicillin allergy and antibiotic sensitivity testing should be requested on the antenatal swab if vancomycin will be needed.

subtopic_detail: Imaging pathway for suspected PE in pregnancy
stem: A 32-year-old G2P1 woman at 24 weeks gestation presents to your ED with sudden onset right-sided pleuritic chest pain and mild breathlessness for 2 hours. She has no calf swelling. There is no haemoptysis. She has no prior VTE history and no family history. Her body mass index is 24. There is no recent travel or surgery.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8, BP 120/76, HR 105, RR 22, SpO2 96 percent RA, PEFR normal
data_table.Examination: Mild tachycardia; chest clear to auscultation; no calf swelling; normal heart sounds; gravid abdomen consistent with dates; foetal heart present 145 bpm
data_table.Investigations: ECG sinus tachycardia, no S1Q3T3; CXR clear bilateral lung fields; FBC normal; arterial blood gas pH 7.43, PaO2 88, PaCO2 36; d-dimer raised at 1200 ng/mL (typical for pregnancy); troponin negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next imaging investigation?

options.text: Bilateral compression ultrasound of lower limb veins, and if negative proceed to ventilation-perfusion (V/Q) scan
options.rationale: AU pathway for suspected PE in pregnancy with normal CXR: bilateral compression USS first (positive USS confirms VTE and avoids further radiation); if negative and PE still suspected, V/Q is preferred over CTPA in pregnancy due to lower maternal breast radiation dose. CTPA preferred if CXR abnormal. (Source: RANZCOG; Thrombosis Australia)
source_refs: Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and NZ

options.text: Computed tomography pulmonary angiogram (CTPA) as first-line investigation given specificity in pregnancy
options.rationale: CTPA is acceptable but V/Q is preferred over CTPA in pregnancy with normal CXR due to lower maternal breast radiation; V/Q has higher foetal radiation but breast cancer risk is the greater concern. (Source: RANZCOG)

options.text: Magnetic resonance pulmonary angiogram (MRPA) as the first-line investigation to avoid radiation exposure
options.rationale: MRPA has limited availability and unclear sensitivity in pregnancy; not first-line. (Source: BMJ Best Practice)

options.text: Bilateral compression ultrasound of lower limb veins as the sole investigation regardless of pulmonary symptoms
options.rationale: Pulmonary embolus can exist without lower limb DVT; further imaging is needed if USS negative. (Source: RANZCOG)

options.text: D-dimer testing alone, with anticoagulation commenced based on the result without further imaging in pregnancy
options.rationale: D-dimer rises physiologically in pregnancy and lacks specificity; cannot be used alone to exclude PE. (Source: RANZCOG)

explanation.summary: VTE is the leading cause of direct maternal mortality in high-income countries. Diagnostic pathway in pregnancy for suspected PE: chest X-ray first to assess for alternative cause, bilateral lower limb compression ultrasound (positive confirms VTE, avoids further radiation), and if negative with persistent suspicion: V/Q scan preferred if CXR clear (lower maternal breast radiation), CTPA preferred if CXR abnormal. D-dimer is not validated in pregnancy. Empiric LMWH if delay to imaging is unavoidable and PE is likely. Treatment: LMWH (enoxaparin 1 mg/kg twice daily) for duration of pregnancy plus 6 weeks postpartum (minimum 3 months treatment).
key_points: Pregnancy: CXR first, then leg USS, then V/Q (if CXR clear) or CTPA (if CXR abnormal)
D-dimer unreliable in pregnancy
LMWH is treatment of choice (warfarin and DOACs contraindicated)
Duration: pregnancy plus 6 weeks postpartum (min 3 months total)
explanation.pearls: V/Q scanning in pregnancy involves around 0.32 mGy foetal dose (below the 50 mGy threshold for foetal harm) but only around 0.02 mGy maternal breast dose; CTPA delivers 0.06 mGy to foetus but 20-50 mGy to maternal breasts - the breast dose is the deciding factor against CTPA in young women with normal CXR.

sources.label: Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and NZ

subtopic_detail: Severe pre-eclampsia with magnesium sulfate prophylaxis
stem: A 28-year-old G1P0 woman at 35 weeks gestation presents to the labour ward with severe occipital headache, blurred vision and right upper quadrant pain. She has been booked under midwifery-led care and has had unremarkable antenatal results until this presentation.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 178/115 (confirmed on 2 readings 15 min apart), HR 88, T 36.8, SpO2 97 percent
data_table.Examination: Hyperreflexia with 4 beats clonus bilateral; severe occipital headache; visual blurring; right upper quadrant tenderness; oedema lower limbs; foetal heart 140 bpm
data_table.Investigations: Urinalysis 3+ protein, PCR 0.42 g/mmol; platelets 95 x10^9/L; ALT 178 U/L; LDH 850; creatinine 92; bilirubin normal; FBC otherwise normal; haptoglobin normal; foetal CTG reassuring

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Administer intravenous magnesium sulfate 4 g loading over 20 minutes then 1 g/hour infusion, with intravenous labetalol for blood pressure control
options.rationale: Severe pre-eclampsia (BP 160/110 or higher with proteinuria, or with severe features: headache, visual disturbance, RUQ pain, hyperreflexia, low platelets, ALT over twice the upper limit) requires immediate IV magnesium sulfate for seizure prevention (4 g loading over 20 min, then 1 g/hour infusion for 24 h or 24 h postpartum), and BP control (IV labetalol or hydralazine, target SBP 140-150 / DBP 90-100). Plan for delivery after stabilisation. (Source: RANZCOG; SOMANZ)

options.text: Administer intravenous dexamethasone 11.4 mg for foetal lung maturity and observe blood pressure overnight without antihypertensive therapy
options.rationale: Steroids alone do not address eclampsia risk; magnesium sulfate is the priority seizure prevention. (Source: SOMANZ)

options.text: Administer oral nifedipine 10 mg immediate release and observe with hourly blood pressure for 6 hours before any further intervention
options.rationale: Oral nifedipine is appropriate for non-severe hypertension; severe pre-eclampsia requires IV agents and magnesium. (Source: SOMANZ)

options.text: Administer intravenous frusemide 40 mg to manage the oedema and reduce blood pressure before any other targeted intervention today
options.rationale: Loop diuretics are contraindicated in pre-eclampsia (reduce placental perfusion) unless pulmonary oedema is present. (Source: SOMANZ)

options.text: Administer oral aspirin 150 mg daily as first-line preventive treatment for pre-eclampsia at this gestation today
options.rationale: Aspirin is preventive (initiated before 16 weeks for high-risk women) not for treating established severe pre-eclampsia. (Source: SOMANZ)

explanation.summary: Severe pre-eclampsia has BP 160/110 or higher, proteinuria PCR 0.30 g/mmol or higher, AND/OR severe features (headache, visual symptoms, RUQ pain, low platelets below 100, ALT over twice ULN, creatinine over 90, hyperreflexia, pulmonary oedema). Management: magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis (4 g loading plus 1 g/hour for 24 hours), IV labetalol or hydralazine for BP, fluid restriction (80 mL/hour total), plan delivery after maternal stabilisation. Magnesium toxicity monitored by deep tendon reflexes, respiratory rate, urine output; calcium gluconate is the antidote. Betamethasone for foetal lung maturity if delivery before 34 weeks. Definitive treatment is delivery.
key_points: Severe features = headache, visual change, RUQ pain, hyperreflexia, low platelets, raised ALT
Magnesium sulfate 4 g load plus 1 g/hour for 24 hours (seizure prophylaxis)
IV labetalol or hydralazine for BP control
Calcium gluconate is the magnesium antidote
explanation.pearls: The MAGPIE trial showed magnesium sulfate halves the risk of eclamptic seizures in pre-eclampsia (NNT around 91 for severe pre-eclampsia, NNT around 36 for imminent eclampsia); it remains underused outside specialist units despite this strong evidence.
